01_initial_request.target_name: Distributed Secondary Voltage and Frequency Restoration Control of Droop-Controlled Inverter-Based Microgrids
01_initial_request.input_data: Islanded inverter-based microgrid with N distributed generations (DGs); each DG includes dc/ac inverter, LCL filter, and RL output connector; droop control parameters (frequency gain k_Pi, voltage gain k_Qi, voltage control gain k_Vi); transmission line impedances (purely inductive assumption); ZIP load model parameters; communication graph among DG controllers.
01_initial_request.user_intent: To address both voltage and frequency restoration in droop-controlled inverter-based islanded microgrids. Existing distributed secondary control methods either address only one variable, require all-to-all communication, or do not consider the inherent coupling between voltage and frequency control. A decoupled distributed control approach is needed that can restore both voltage and frequency simultaneously without a central controller.
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: 1) Restore all DG voltage magnitudes V_i to reference value V^d using finite-time voltage control; 2) Restore system frequency ω to reference ω^d using consensus-based frequency control; 3) Maintain accurate real power sharing; 4) Achieve decoupled voltage and frequency control design; 5) Implement fully distributed without central controller.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Each DG in the microgrid has a droop-controlled inverter with dynamics: δ̇_i = ω^d - k_Pi(P^m_i - P^d_i) for frequency, and k_ViV̇_i = (V^d - V_i) - k_Qi(Q^m_i - Q^d_i) for voltage. The hierarchical control structure has primary (droop), secondary (restoration), and tertiary (optimization) layers. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The inherently existing coupling between frequency and voltage in the MG dynamics has not been properly addressed in existing distributed control designs. Most methods either ignore voltage restoration or assume constant node voltage. [Decision] To achieve decoupled control design, first analyzed the DG and network models under the purely inductive transmission line assumption. Derived a simplified dynamic model of the islanded MG that reveals the structural properties enabling voltage-frequency decoupling.

parameters.dg_model: Droop control: δ̇_i = ω^d - k_Pi(P^m_i - P^d_i), k_ViV̇_i = (V^d - V_i) - k_Qi(Q^m_i - Q^d_i)
parameters.network_model: Purely inductive transmission lines: P_i = V_iV_j|Y_ij|sin(δ_i-δ_j), Q_i related to voltage magnitudes
parameters.zip_load_model: P_Li = P_1iV_i^2 + P_2iV_i + P_3i, Q_Li = Q_1iV_i^2 + Q_2iV_i + Q_3i
parameters.simplification: Under inductive line assumption, real power P mainly depends on phase angle δ, reactive power Q mainly depends on voltage V

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Under the purely inductive transmission line assumption, the MG dynamics exhibit a natural decoupling: real power is primarily governed by phase angle differences (frequency), while reactive power is primarily governed by voltage magnitude differences. This structural property enables designing voltage and frequency controllers separately.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The DG and network models have been derived, revealing natural P-δ/Q-V decoupling under inductive line assumption. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: A voltage controller must be designed that can restore all DG voltage magnitudes to the reference value in finite time, enabling decoupled frequency control design. [Decision] To achieve finite-time voltage restoration, designed a distributed finite-time voltage controller using the terminal sliding mode approach. The controller for each DG uses neighbor voltage information: u_{Vi} = -k_1·sign(s_i) - k_2·s_i, where s_i = Σ_{j∈N_i}a_{ij}(V_i - V_j) + b_i(V_i - V^d).

parameters.convergence_time: Finite time determined by controller gains and initial conditions
parameters.pinning_term: b_i > 0 for DGs that can access reference voltage V^d

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The finite-time voltage controller ensures all DG voltage magnitudes converge to the reference V^d within a finite time T* that can be computed from the controller gains. Critically, this finite-time convergence is independent of the frequency control, enabling the voltage and frequency controllers to be designed separately — the key decoupling insight.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Finite-time voltage controller has been designed, ensuring V_i → V^d in finite time, which decouples voltage from frequency control. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: A frequency controller must be designed to restore system frequency to ω^d while maintaining real power sharing accuracy, subject to the constraint that control inputs should be equal at steady state. [Decision] To achieve frequency restoration with power sharing, designed a consensus-based distributed frequency controller using a proportional-integral approach. The controller combines frequency restoration and power sharing: u_{fi} = -k_f(ω_i - ω^d) + k_pΣa_{ij}(P_i - P_j) - k_I·∫[Σa_{ij}(u_{fi} - u_{fj})]dt, with input saturation to handle control constraints.

parameters.frequency_restoration_term: -k_f(ω_i - ω^d) for DGs with reference access
parameters.power_sharing_term: k_p·Σa_{ij}(P_i - P_j) for accurate power sharing
parameters.input_constraint: Saturation function to limit control input magnitude

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The consensus-based frequency controller simultaneously drives frequency to ω^d and maintains real power sharing. The integral consensus term ensures all frequency control inputs converge to the same value, which is the key to preserving the droop-control-established power sharing. A sufficient condition for frequency restoration with bounded control inputs is established through Lyapunov analysis.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Both voltage (finite-time) and frequency (consensus-based) controllers have been designed with decoupled architecture. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The combined system stability must be rigorously analyzed, and the decoupling property must be formally proven to ensure the controllers work correctly together. [Decision] To establish system stability, first proved that the finite-time voltage controller drives V_i → V^d in finite time, after which the voltage dynamics become stationary. Then analyzed the frequency control stability separately under the condition V_i = V^d.

parameters.voltage_stability: Finite-time convergence proved via terminal sliding mode theory
parameters.frequency_stability: Lyapunov analysis under condition V_i = V^d (after voltage convergence)
parameters.decoupling_condition: When V_i = V^d for all i, frequency dynamics become independent of voltage variations
parameters.sufficient_condition: Conditions on k_f, k_p, k_I involving graph connectivity eigenvalues

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The decoupled stability analysis proves: 1) Voltage converges to V^d in finite time regardless of frequency dynamics; 2) After voltage convergence, frequency dynamics can be analyzed independently; 3) A sufficient local stability condition is established for the combined system. This formally justifies the decoupled control design approach.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Theoretical analysis has established the decoupled stability of the combined voltage and frequency controllers. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The proposed control strategies must be validated through numerical simulation on a realistic microgrid test system. [Decision] To validate the proposed approach, built an islanded MG test system with 4 DGs in MATLAB and conducted simulation studies testing voltage restoration, frequency restoration, power sharing, load changes, and communication topology variations.

parameters.test_system: 4 DG islanded microgrid with inverter-based DGs
parameters.test_scenarios: Voltage restoration, frequency restoration, load step changes, DG plug-in/out
parameters.comparison: Compared with centralized control and single-variable restoration methods

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Simulation results confirm: 1) All DG voltage magnitudes converge to reference V^d within finite time (~0.3s); 2) System frequency is restored to ω^d; 3) Accurate real power sharing is maintained; 4) The decoupled design works correctly — voltage control does not interfere with frequency control and vice versa; 5) The distributed implementation avoids single-point failures inherent in centralized control.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Rigorous theoretical analysis with finite-time convergence proof for voltage control and Lyapunov stability analysis for frequency control, validated through MATLAB simulation on a 4-DG islanded microgrid test system.

Voltage Restoration Time.interpretation: All DG voltage magnitudes converge to reference value within finite time, as guaranteed by the terminal sliding mode design.

Frequency Restoration Accuracy.interpretation: System frequency is restored to the reference value with zero steady-state error under the sufficient stability condition.

Real Power Sharing Error.interpretation: Accurate real power sharing is maintained after both voltage and frequency restoration.

Decoupling Effectiveness.interpretation: Voltage and frequency controllers can be designed independently — finite-time voltage convergence ensures no interference with frequency dynamics.

Distributed Architecture Advantage.interpretation: Only neighbor-to-neighbor communication is required, avoiding single-point failures and reducing communication infrastructure costs.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The proposed distributed secondary control strategy successfully achieves both voltage and frequency restoration in droop-controlled inverter-based microgrids. The key innovation is the decoupled design enabled by finite-time voltage control: by first driving all voltages to the reference in finite time, the frequency controller can be designed independently. Both simulation and theoretical analysis confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
